key_insight: A drilled-to-bossed joint geometry combined with optimized DDFW settings substantially improves the weld strength of AA2024-T351, while deep learning can predict tensile performance from process parameters.
engineering_relevance: The study offers a practical route to stronger lightweight aluminum joints for aerospace and automotive applications, where reliable high-strength welding is critical.
method: Used an L18 orthogonal array to test friction pressure, forging pressure, spindle speed, faying-surface geometry, and friction time.
Compared conventional flat-to-flat joints with a novel drilled-to-bossed geometry inspired by mortise-and-tenon mechanics.
Trained deep learning models, including an ensemble residual network, to predict ultimate tensile strength from welding conditions.
evidence_signal: The best condition achieved 538 MPa tensile strength and 325 MPa torsional strength, and the top model reached about R² = 0.81.
watch_item: The dataset appears limited to a designed experimental matrix, so model generalization beyond the tested welding window and material setup may be constrained.
paper_title: Enhancing weld performance of AA2024-T351 using drilled-to-bossed geometry and deep learning prediction in direct drive friction welding
